data.title: BJ's Restaurants: Hot Pizookies, Frozen Pipeline
data.description:
Pizookie Meal Deal Arrived to Save the Day

It appears that BJ's Restaurants (NASDAQ:BJRI) is gradually relearning how to drive traffic in a market where guests are seeking bargains. It hasn’t been a walk in the park. From Pizookie to Pizookie, BJ’s managed to report better-than-expected results for the fourth quarter.

Since its ill-fated foray into value platforms with the Pizookie Pass, the full-service restaurant chain has gone back to basics. It launched a prix fixe offering for lunch, offering affordable price points for guests looking for a convenient and tasty meal.

There are about eight options of different dishes, ranging from Italian pasta with chicken parmigiana to a mini Deep Deep pizza with house salad. But of course, the Pizookie couldn't be left out. The difference with the price platform is that this iconic dessert from BJ's Restaurants is already included in the price of $13.

But wait a minute! It's important to note that the Pizookie served in the promotion is the personal Pizookie, which is about half the size of a regular Pizookie.

If you think it’s not worth it, think again.

If we ordered the items separately and at the regular menu price, the promotional items would come out to an average of $14. Combine that with the price of the Pizookie and you’d be spending about $22 on this lunch. With the special offer, we’re getting a discount of about $9!

We had some color on how this offering performed since early September. BJ's Restaurants said they outperformed Black Box Casual Dining by approximately 500 basis points on average, even reporting same-store sales up 4% year-over-year in the first few weeks of October.

But it doesn’t stop there. In Q4 FY 2024, BJ’s Restaurants reported same-store sales up 5.5% year over year, likely picking up momentum in November, a much higher-than-average month for the industry. Although it didn’t disclose it, BJ’s saw weaker comps for traffic, which had been declining year-over-year since Q2 FY 2023 but began to decline in the mid-single digits in Q4 FY 2023.

I said that BJ’s Restaurants had likely seen weaker comps ahead and that we had seen a bottom in same-store sales. That appears to be exactly what happened.

In short, these three factors were the biggest drivers of same-store sales for Q4:

  1. Pizookie Meal Deal attracting bargain-hunting guests to full-service restaurants. Note that the promotion was ideal for dessert lovers, with significant discounts but higher prices;
  2. A particularly strong quarter and well above the restaurant industry average for the biennium comprising 2023 and 2024. You probably saw this in the Restaurant Recap; and
  3. Softer comparisons, but mainly traffic. They stopped growing year-over-year five periods ago and have been declining ever since. BJ's Restaurants tries to compensate for this drop in traffic by increasing prices, which are losing their effectiveness now. Targeting traffic seemed like the ideal strategy now.

Let's look at the graph to better understand this:

Moving on to smaller drivers, we had the Spooky Pizookie. This was a seasonal dessert from BJ's Restaurants that returned after customer feedback. We know that Halloween 2024 was a boom time for restaurants, right in the middle of October and November. Even Papa John's (PZZA) reported that October 31st itself was its highest-transaction day in the brand's history within the United States.

Speaking of beers, it's a wonderful subject, especially when you're thirsty, isn't it? Management noted that they saw tailwinds during the 25th anniversary celebration of their Belgian Grand Cru beer.

This is an award-winning label, having won the World Beer Cup in 2000 and another silver at the Great American Beer Festival in 2002. And as part of the celebration, BJ's is selling a limited-edition silver collector's cup. This ended up making a bit of a splash, especially since this is one of BJ's Restaurants' most famous handcrafted beers.

I know you liked my trivia. Here's another one! In the first six years of its existence, BJ's changed the recipe for Grand Cru for at least six years. This is in line with the Belgian tradition of changing recipes for its handcrafted beers from time to time. With customers clamoring for the award-winning version from the early 2000s, BJ's has stuck with the original recipe for at least eight years.

Pipeline Continues Slow, But Has Undergone Strategic Reviews

I'm sure many of you, like me, thought that BJ's Restaurants would open a few restaurants in FY 2024. Well, the Brookfield opening was going very well, generating reasonably good sales volume for a low DMA area. But nothing happened. That's a shame.

On the bright side, BJ's has conducted a strategic review of its restaurant portfolio and has not identified any locations that need to close. All of them are generating relatively healthy AUVs with restaurant-level margins in the mid-teens. Looking ahead, the company has decided to postpone some openings that were planned for FY 2025, reducing the potential for openings next year. They now expect to open one location year-round (Queens Creek, Arizona). The expectation is that this will pick up in FY 2026.

This is understandable. BJ's restaurant-level economics are not the best, despite short-term growth. Restaurant-level margins are 15.4%, up 100 basis points year over year. Generating AUVs of $7 million, these boxes generate a very attractive unit-level cash flow of approximately $1.08 million per year. The problem here is the same as other upscale casual dining restaurants that have high construction costs.

If you don't know, BJ's restaurants are quite expensive to build. Before the Lean Prototype, which maintains annual sales of $7 million but has a construction cost of $6 million, BJ's Restaurants was working with $7 million prototypes. That's quite a hefty sum, especially for an AUV of this size.

The implications are clear. When BJ's Restaurants opens a store, they expect to recoup their capital investment in the 5th or 6th year of operation. And I'm not even talking about the discounted payback! You could argue that Portillo's (PTLO) or Cheesecake Factory (CAKE) are more expensive per case, but look at the sales volume of those companies. Each Portillo's location in Chicagoland has AUVs of over $12 million, and the same can be said for Cheesecake Factory.

Understanding the Impact on BJ's Margins

For some time now, I have been reporting that, despite being emptier, BJ's restaurants were more economical, especially after the entry of PW Partners, an activist investor. Well, this was the strategy adopted by many other restaurants to deal with the decline in consumption. Even El Pollo Loco (LOCO) joined in. However, many of the benefits of these initiatives could not be seen while the company saw its top-line sales decline year after year. It was necessary, more than ever, to boost its sales. And that happened. Here are the improvements.

Back to basics, same-store sales across the board increased 5.5% year over year during Q4. Consider that about 100 basis points of that was driven by favorable holiday shifts. Still, 4.5% year-over-year growth isn’t something you see every day at a restaurant chain these days.

Since we had no recent restaurant openings, top-line sales grew 6.4% year-over-year to $344.3 million during Q4 FY 2024. Now, looking at the full year, since other periods BJ's Restaurants saw negative same-store sales, top-line sales grew just 1.8% year-over-year to $1.357 billion. Note that this was driven solely by same-store sales growth, as restaurant openings were not a significant driver here.

Note that now, with top-line sales growing, fixed costs have ended up being better diluted, becoming a little less relevant in relation to revenue. But consider that these fixed costs are already being reduced as the company implements its cost reduction initiatives.

Here are some of them:

  • AI-based workforce forecasting;
  • Optimization of complimentary food and beverage management;
  • Simplification of margarita registration at the POS (decreased wrong orders by 20%);
  • Improved order display in the kitchen for pickup and delivery (increased accuracy by 10%);
  • Implemented QR codes to track repairs and predict replacements;
  • Adopted preventive maintenance for kitchen equipment;
  • Deepened the shoulder-to-shoulder training in the back-of-the-house.

This not only directly reduced costs, but also helped reduce turnover among hourly employees and GMs. You may already know how reducing this metric is related to both increased traffic and same-store sales. BBI data suggests that restaurants that are fully staffed perform better. For GMs, the benefit is 2.8% year-over-year for traffic and 3.3% year-over-year for same-store sales. For hourly employees, the impact is even greater, 3.6% year-over-year for traffic and 4.6% year-over-year for same-store sales.

Virtually every cost center benefited except Food, Beverage and Packaging, which grew 40 basis points year over year and now accounts for 25.9% of total revenue. Note that this was driven by the impact of the Pizookie Meal Deal. There are eight different dishes, with costs modestly higher than the rest of the BJ's menu. We anticipated this, but it appears that the price increases have made this impact less significant.

Labor costs were 35.8%, down 70 basis points year-over-year, impacted by positive labor initiatives. They are still high, about 500 basis points above the ideal and at least 300 above the acceptable. In theory, with same-store sales growing, we will see these costs gradually being diluted, but in my opinion there is a long way to go until normality, even with wages in the industry growing at a slower pace (except for chefs).

These changes resulted in full-year EBITDA reaching $104.8 million in FY 2024, a margin of 7.72%. This is up from 6.96% in FY 2023. EBIT also grew 48.4% year-over-year to $32.5 million, with a margin of 2.39%. All of these metrics are far above what BJ's has achieved in the post-pandemic period. However, they fall short when compared to the period prior to FY 2019.

Debt and Buybacks: What Will BJ's Do With the Spare Cash?

BJ's Restaurants ended FY 2024 with long-term debt of $66.5 million and cash and equivalents of $26.1 million. Note that they deleveraged their balance sheet with $7.7 million of debt, using a higher OCF, even with a higher Capex to pay down debt. This gives us a Debt/EBITDA ratio of 2.04x. Nothing to worry about.

Also taking advantage of stronger cash flow, BJ's Restaurants repurchased 234,000 shares of common stock at a cost of $8 million. They currently have $83 million available in our share repurchase program after recently increasing the program by $50 million. They currently have 22.9 million shares outstanding.

Note that at this point, if they execute the full repurchase program, BJ's could reduce their shares outstanding by at least 2 million. However, we will see higher capex during FY 2025 due to remodeling. They plan to remodel up to 30 existing locations.

Guidance for FY 2025

  • Q1 comparable sales up close to 2%. Note that this still leaves BJ's outperforming the BBI Index for the quarter, but narrows the spread due to the weather in certain markets and the more general conservatism in consumer spending after the holidays. Management expects comps to accelerate during Q2 and Q3, but moderate during Q4 as they face this quarter. My view is that they should remain in the low single digits for Q1, slowly accelerating to mid-single digits by mid-year and perhaps a flatter Q4;
  • Adjusted EBITDA of $127 million to $137 million, representing 21.2% year-over-year growth;
  • Capital expenditures of $65 million to $75 million. In 2025, they plan to open one new restaurant and remodel up to 30 existing locations. Note that subtracting the $6 million for the new opening, that’s $1.9 million spent per restaurant on remodeling. By the end of 2025, approximately 60% of BJ’s restaurants will be recent prototypes;
  • Repurchase $40 million to $50 million of shares in 2025. If the price remains in the mid-$30s, BJ's will buy back 1.1 million to 1.4 million shares, reducing BJ's outstanding shares to 21 million.

Should I Buy This Growth Or Is It Expensive?

If we strip out non-cash items, we have EPS of $1.40. Note that this is quite different from the $0.72 EPS that GAAP tells us. Given an analyst consensus EPS of $1.67, we have a projected 19.3% year-over-year growth. That's still good, but much lower than the 131.9% year-over-year growth that the raw numbers suggest.

I say this because many may look at the PEG of 0.3 and get excited. But when we consider growth based on non-GAAP standards, we get a projected PEG that varies from 1.28 to 1.74. Therefore, I do not see EPS growth as a relevant reason to recommend buying here. On the contrary, this PEG is 20.66% higher than the industry median.

Let's make some projections based on your EPS and RPS. Remember, I'm using the Modigliani-Miller Hypothesis for Dividend Irrelevance to Firm Value to model the scenarios in the DDM:

  1. I am considering projected EPS of $1.67 for FY 2025, this will be our first cash flow to shareholders;
  2. I am projecting top-line sales of $1.4 billion for FY 2025. If I expect shares outstanding to be 21.7 million, the RPS would be $64.51;
  3. BJ's Restaurants' 5-year average P/S is 0.67. Assuming the stock trades at this level through FY 2025, we have an exit price of $43.22. This will be our second cash flow to the shareholder;
  4. The two cash flows discounted to present value under a Cost of Equity of 7.62%, we have a PT of $41.71.

That said, I expect BJ's Restaurants to continue trading in the high $30s to low $40s. Cost containment initiatives will continue to pay off, but I consider the stock still expensive for a restaurant chain that will open just one location during FY 2025 and is expected to maintain same-store sales in the low single digits year over year.

I maintain my 'Hold' rating on BJ's Restaurants with a PT of around $41. We will continue to monitor how the price behaves after the earnings release. If it rises dramatically above our PT, we may take profits. However, if it falls below the low $30s, I see room for buying. But beyond that, there are better options out there for buy-and-hold.

data.title: Arteris Q4: Long Growth Runway Ahead Overshadowed By The Lack Of Profitability
data.description:
Investment thesis

Shares of Arteris (NASDAQ:AIP) are up more than 20% since I initiated coverage on the stock last October. I had previously walked through its interconnect technology for Network on Chips ("NoC") and growth outlook for the business, concluding by assigning its shares a Buy rating. The company maintained its growth rate of 16% in Annual Contract Value ("ACV") plus royalties in Q4, coupled with improving profitability. Despite securing key design wins with large customers, the company remains largely underfollowed, which could change once it reaches greater scale and garners more attention like other companies that are benefitting from the increased adoption of AI. With growth set to stay in the high teens in the coming years and margins continuing to move higher, shares appear attractively valued given the large discount compared to its peers. Given its higher risk profile, I maintain AIP stock as a speculative Buy.

Key takeaways from Q4 earnings and guidance

When looking at the company's results, investors should understand that ACV plus Royalties and Remaining Performance Obligations ("RPO") are the key metrics to track growth, instead of Revenue, given the ratable nature of how revenue is recognized. For Q4, ACV plus Royalties ended at $65.1 million, up 16% year over year, as shown above. Management's 2025 guidance calls for ACV plus Royalties to reach between $73 million and $77 million, implying growth of just over 15% at the midpoint. The trend in RPO, which leads ACV plus Royalties remains strong, growing 22% year over year.

Turning to profitability, management has guided for FCF of between $1 million and $7 million in 2025, despite having a cash burn of $1 million in FY24. The weaker FCF in Q4 can be explained by significantly higher Accounts Receivables, leading to unfavorable working capital movements. With positive FCF expected going forward, the company's financial position remains solid with cash of $43.8 million and debt of $2.1 million.

On a non-GAAP basis, losses are expected to be between $8.5 million and $12 million for FY25. Given the current growth outlook, there is a clear path for an inflection to non-GAAP profitability next year.

Reasons that keep me bullish on the story

Since Arteris is still unprofitable and early in its growth journey, it is important to understand its long-term growth story and the associated growth drivers. While I outlined several of the company's growth drivers in my previous write-up, including royalties which are set up to ramp up significantly in the medium term, I will highlight some recent developments which prove that the long-term growth story remains well intact.

On the Q4 earnings call, management stated that the latest FlexGen Smart NoC IP was capable of delivering up to ten times more engineering productivity, while also lowering power consumption. Most importantly for investors, they have a 30% higher average selling price.

Management also noted that its technology was increasingly being used for chiplets, due to the technology’s superior power and performance, with a key customer Tenstorrent, expanding its deployment of Arteris NoC in its next-generation solutions. The chiplet market was valued at $6.7 billion in 2023, and was forecasted to grow at a 73% CAGR between 2024 and 2032, fueled by growth in AI and high-performance computing.

A notable customer win that was highlighted in Q3 was NIO (NIO), a Chinese EV manufacturer growing revenue at over 20%. Arteris technology has been chosen to be used in NIO's ADAS and LiDAR systems that will be incorporated in its future vehicles. NIO is among nine other automotive OEM customers that work with Arteris.

Given these positives, shares of Arteris trade at an EV/(ACV plus Royalties) multiple of just 4.5, based on the midpoint of 2025 guidance. This represents a large discount compared to relevant peers such as Cadence Design (CDNS) and Synopsys (SNPS) which have growth rates in the low-teens, and are valued at EV/Revenue multiples of 13 and 11 respectively.

While a valuation discount is warranted due to its higher risk profile given its smaller scale and lack of profitability, I believe that the market is overlooking the fact that the company is set up well to consistently grow at high-teen rates in the coming years, surpassing $100 million in ACV plus royalties in FY27, with FCF margins greater than 20% achievable in the long term, in line with its peers. The business should continue to show strong operating leverage, with annual expense growth likely to remain in the high single digits.

Risks to consider

Arteris has a strong moat due to the patents it possesses and has high switching costs since its technology is ingrained within the customers' designs. However, it is highly dependent on certain large customers and the success of their respective product rollouts. For instance, lower shipments by Mobileye (MBLY) last year negatively impacted Arteris's revenue from royalties. Delays or cancellations in specific customer projects could significantly impact its growth and margin outlook. According to its latest annual report, more than half its Accounts receivable was concentrated among three major customers.

Another concern for investors is Arteris's lack of profitability. While the business is set to be FCF positive this year, with a line of sight to achieve non-GAAP profitability next year, GAAP profitability is still further away since stock-based compensation ("SBC") expenses remain very high, at close to $16 million annually, as shown above. Overall dilution has been close to 10% a year over the past two years, and this represents a headwind for future shareholder returns.

AIP remains a Speculative Buy

While the company's long-term growth story remains on track as it moves closer to achieving positive FCF, there are certain risks which temper my optimism. While AIP stock is still valued at a significant discount versus its peers, shares could remain highly volatile, leading me to stick to recommending it as a speculative Buy, with a carefully sized allocation to reflect its inherent risks.

Fund performance

Columbia Disciplined Large Value Fund Class A shares (MUTF:RLCAX) returned -0.92% (excluding sales charge) and its Institutional shares returned -0.96% for the quarter ended December 31, 2024. The fund posted solid negative absolute returns but outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000 Value Index (Russell Index), which returned -1.98% for the same period. The fund’s results can be attributed primarily to the relative performance of its model’s stock selection measures.

Market overview

The U.S. stock market gained 2.75% in the fourth quarter, based on the large-cap Russell 1000 Index, bringing its return for the full year to 24.51%. The November election result removed a long-standing source of uncertainty for investors, and it raised hopes that government policy would shift in a more pro-growth, market-friendly direction. In addition, U.S. equities continued to attract investor capital as the nation’s economic growth remained well above that of its global developed-market peers. These factors helped propel stocks to a positive return despite indications that the U.S. Federal Reserve is unlikely to cut interest rates significantly in the year ahead.

The shift in expectations toward a less accommodative Fed played a role in the continued outperformance of growth stocks. As was the case throughout 2024, U.S. mega-cap technology companies—particularly those expected to capitalize on the increased adoption of artificial intelligence—remained the primary driver of both broader market returns and relative strength of the growth style. The Russell 1000 Growth Index returned 7.07% in the quarter, outpacing the -1.98% return of the Russell 1000 Value Index. The two indices returned 33.36% and 14.37%, respectively, for the full year.

In another continuation of a trend that was in place in the year’s first three quarters, large-cap stocks outperformed mid-caps (as measured by the 0.62% return of the Russell MidCap Index) and small caps (as measured by the 0.33% return of the Russell 2000 Index). Smaller companies are typically more rate-sensitive than large caps, and they benefited less from the robust inflows into passive investments.

Model performance

We divide the metrics for our stock selection model into three broad categories — quality, value and catalyst. We then rank the securities within a sector/industry from “1” (most attractive) to “5” (least attractive) based upon the metrics within these categories. During the fourth quarter of 2024, the catalyst theme contributed positively to relative results, while the quality and value themes detracted. Three of the four factors within the catalyst theme scored positively during the quarter, led by the best-performing factor overall, cash flow surprise. The earnings revision and 12-month price momentum factors also performed well, with relative strength the only catalyst factor to score negatively during the quarter. The quality theme of our stock selection model had mixed performance, with three of its factors contributing positively and three detracting. The change in free-cash- flow margin and cash-flow-return on assets factors performed well, while the return-on- equity and return-on-invested-capital factors weighed on results. The value theme of our stock selection model was weakest, with all eight of its factors dampening relative results during the quarter. Dividend yield, earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization (EBITDA)-to-enterprise value and forward earnings/price were the largest detractors within the value theme and overall.

Among risk factors, volatility and beta were the largest positive contributors to relative results for the quarter, while financial leverage and size were the largest detractors.

Of the portfolio’s 20 industry-specific models, 11 provided positive stock selection guidance during the fourth quarter. Consumer discretionary-retail & apparel, utilities and information technology-software contributed most positively to relative results. Consumer discretionary-autos & durables, energy and industrials-services detracted the most during the quarter.

As usual, the portfolio maintained a relatively neutral stance on sector allocation, though sector allocation did contribute positively, albeit modestly, to relative performance during the quarter. Stock selection overall contributed most positively to the portfolio’s performance relative to the Russell Index. Stock selection was strongest in the utilities, health care and materials sectors. Partially offsetting these positive contributors was weak stock selection in energy, industrials and consumer staples, which detracted.

Among the top detractors from relative performance were:

  • PulteGroup, a homebuilding company, experienced a double-digit share price decline during the quarter. The homebuilding industry broadly pulled back in the last months of 2024, as demand trends had yet to improve meaningfully and mortgage rates moved higher, both serving as headwinds. The portfolio’s overweight in PulteGroup was based on positive scores across all three themes — quality, value and catalyst— but the models delivered negative stock selection guidance.
  • Tenet Healthcare, a diversified health care services company, posted strong results for the third quarter of 2024, but its stock fell in the fourth quarter due to a combination of factors, including rising operating costs and concerns about potential government spending cuts affecting hospital profits. The portfolio’s overweight in Tenet Healthcare was driven by its attractive quality, value and catalyst theme scores, but the models provided negative stock selection guidance.

Among the top positive contributors to relative performance:

  • Salesforce, a leading customer relationship management technology company that provides customer relationship management solutions, posted a double-digit share price gain during the quarter, supported by better-than-consensus expected revenue guidance for the first quarter of 2025. The company continued to focus on margin improvement, Artificial Intelligence expansion and earnings growth. The portfolio’s overweight in Salesforce was based on its attractive scores across all three themes — value, catalyst and quality, and the models delivered positive stock selection guidance.
  • Ralph Lauren, which designs, markets and distributes fashion products, enjoyed a double-digit share price increase during the quarter on improved foot traffic at its stores and its reports of better-than-consensus expected earnings and increased revenue. The portfolio’s overweight in Ralph Lauren was driven by positive scores across all three themes — value, catalyst and quality, and the models provided positive stock selection guidance.

Sector weights (%): fund vs. benchmark as of December 31, 2024

During the fourth quarter, certain enhancements were made to our quantitative stock selection models to improve their relevance in uncertain markets. Most notably, the adjusted book-to-price factor was enhanced to now use combined trailing and forward return on equity data. Extreme factor values are managed by applying percentile ranks pre-regression. (Regression models are a tool for predictive analytics used in quantitative modeling.)

Outlook

For the second consecutive year, U.S. equity markets rallied substantially, with the S&P 500 Index hitting 57 new all-time highs in 2024 alone. Given this strong run in the markets, the foremost question on investors’ minds, in our view, is whether the rally still has legs or if there will be causes for it to stall. We believe the actions of the incoming U.S. administration, the Federal Reserve’s interest-rate policy and the resilience of the consumer, among other drivers, will likely determine which direction the markets take.

The new year will bring a new administration to Washington, D.C., and with it, substantial changes in government policy. With support in both the House and the Senate, there appears to be a higher chance the administration can deliver on many of its promises. An emphasis on deregulation and a focus on economic growth and energy production are likely, in our opinion, to be tailwinds to corporate profitability. A friendlier approach to corporate mergers and acquisitions may also be a positive for the equity markets. On the other hand, the incoming President’s focus on tariffs, especially with close trading partners Canada and Mexico, could dampen economic activity and increase inflation. Potential trade wars can create more friction to global commerce. Investors will almost surely continue to watch if this is a serious policy proposal or just a negotiating tactic.

The Fed, having navigated the historic rise in inflation with aggressive interest rate increases, began easing rates in September 2024 and seems to have successfully avoided a hard economic landing. As the last mile of the inflation reduction journey remains challenging, Fed policymakers have been reconsidering the pace and trajectory of interest rate cuts for the months ahead. However, despite inflation remaining stubborn at around 3%, there also seems to be consensus among Fed officials that the neutral rate — or the interest rate that would prevail when the economy is at full employment and inflation is stable — is lower than current interest rates. So, we believe we are likely to see interest rate cuts in 2025 that can support equity markets.

High levels of and rising delinquency rates in consumer debt and auto loans could be a cause for concern, in our view, but spending has remained robust, and lower interest rates should provide additional cushion to shore up the health of the consumer. Similarly, in the housing market, where high mortgage rates have significantly affected the supply of existing homes for sale, we feel a reduction in rates should catalyze more transactions and continue to support this sector of the economy. The labor market remained in balance at the end of 2024, with unemployment hovering around 4%. Despite these positives, with the economy in 2024 growing at a faster clip than consensus expectations, we believe a slight slowdown is likely in the next year.

One more theme we believe will continue to be important going forward is the potential of Artificial Intelligence adoption to transform businesses. The largest companies have invested hundreds of billions of dollars in capital expenditure to compete for new revenue opportunities. As the returns from these investments become clearer, markets may well reward those with the best strategy and execution. Geopolitical risk also remains a concern for the U.S. equity markets, with conflicts in the Middle East and Ukraine still simmering.

The effects of government policy, Fed actions and other market drivers are likely, in our opinion, to be uneven across industries, and foreseeing which market segments will lead or lag can be challenging. Just as we observed in the fourth quarter of 2024, there could be wide dispersion among the prospects of different economic sectors. Focusing on stock selection and staying relatively sector neutral can mitigate some performance volatility.

Relying on fundamentals to identify those companies that can execute on their strategies and post solid earnings and cash flows can also be a potential benefit to portfolio results. data.title: Match Group: A Value Trap With Worrying Drop In Paid Users (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
With the stock market continuing to hover near all-time highs, it’s an excellent time for investors to deploy careful stock picking and ditch lower-quality stocks, particularly those with trouble achieving significant growth. In my view, we’re due for a 10-15% correction this year, and fundamentally weak stocks will get pounded disproportionately.

Match Group (NASDAQ:MTCH), the parent company behind Tinder and Hinge and the largest portfolio of dating apps in the market, unfortunately sits in this category. The company has continued to bleed users, rendering its longer-term financial targets appear quite unachievable. Yet year to date, shares of Match have risen modestly even after a terrible Q4 earnings print - which in my view may deepen the potential correction in this name.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Match in December, when the stock was trading closer to $31 per share. I had based my upgrade on the company’s Q3 trend of adding paid users to Tinder, its most important and lucrative property. Since then, Tinder has sharply bled users, while paid adds for Match’s major growth vehicle, Hinge, has also slowed. Amid a ~5% rise in the stock over the last two months, I’m downgrading Match back down to a sell rating.

As a reminder, Match has laid out an aggressive financial plan in its 2024 investor day. The key tenets of this plan, through FY27, include 4-6% annual revenue growth (buoyed by ~20% growth for Hinge and a return to low single digit growth for Tinder, which is currently in decline) and $3+ billion of cumulative FCF for a three-year period.

And yet, we think the company’s likelihood of meeting these targets is low, especially given recent trends. The core red flags to watch out for here are:

  • Tinder keeps losing users. The company has worked tremendously hard to rebrand Tinder as a more serious dating app, and has implemented stricter account creation standards as well as requiring user biometrics to increase the perceived quality of daters on the platform. Whether this strategy will work in the long term is unknown, but for now, it’s causing Tinder’s paid users to plummet.
  • Match competes against itself. Match operates a huge portfolio of apps, and unlike conglomerates with synergistic businesses, the company’s bestselling products actually cannibalize each other. Hinge is succeeding, but largely at the expense of Tinder.

All in all, I find it unlikely that Match can regain its footing. Sell this stock and invest elsewhere.

Q4 download

Let’s now go through Match’s latest quarterly results in greater detail to demonstrate where the company is hurting most. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Match’s revenue declined -1% y/y to $860.2 million, barely edging out over Wall Street’s expectations of $857.6 million (also -1% y/y). The company notes that FX continues to be a headwind, with the stronger dollar hitting the company’s Latin America and Asia segments. We note that international expansion is one of the company’s growth pillars for this year, with plans to expand Hinge into Mexico and Brazil in 2H’25. But unfortunately, the company is expecting conditions to get worse before they get better: the company’s guidance for $820-$830 million in revenue for Q1 implies a -4% y/y decline at the midpoint.

The key issue here is a struggle to maintain paid users. The trended schedule below showcases that Match lost 0.6 million net paid users in Q4:

This was driven primarily by a 554k decline in Tinder payers. I had hope for a resurgence in Tinder on the back of the company’s new account-creation policies in Q3, when the app temporarily saw a burst in new paid signups. The company’s latest Q4 count of 9.49 million payers, however, is now below even Q2’s paid user base (meaning all the net adds that Tinder enjoyed in Q3 have been more than washed out), and the Tinder payer base is sitting at multi-year lows.

Adding insult to injury is the fact that Hinge’s growth has also stalled. Hinge added only 17k payers in Q4, far below the 118k it added in Q3 and half the 35k it added in the year-ago Q4. Hinge’s main tagline is that it’s the dating app designed to be deleted - and so it may find itself now a victim of its own success in finding matches.

It’s important to note that acceleration in Hinge revenue is key to Match’s ability to meet its outlook. Per new CFO Steve Bailey’s remarks during the Q&A portion of the Q4 earnings call:

Aside from Tinder, we also expect other brands to deliver improved revenue growth throughout the year as they execute on their product roadmaps and deliver their monetization initiatives. In particular, we assume that Hinge’s revenue growth strengthens in the second half of the year as user growth continues to be strong and they execute on their product roadmap. So collectively between what Tinder's expect to deliver, what the other brands, including Hinge, expects to deliver, and the solid start to new users to start the year, we're confident we can deliver gradually improving year-over-year total revenue growth throughout the year and achieve our full year total revenue outlook.”

But this path looks uncertain, especially as Hinge’s payer growth slowed to 19% y/y in Q4. The other lever that Match has is average revenue per payer (RPP) growth, which clocked in at 8% y/y growth to $30.42 in Q4, nearly twice as high as Tinder. But with planned international expansion later this year, RPP growth may also slow as international users tend to generate less per head (not to mention FX risks). Meanwhile, we note that the company’s other two smaller segments, Evergreen & Emerging (which includes more niche dating apps aimed at specific sub-groups) and Match Group Asia are also losing payers.

Meanwhile, we note that profitability is also trending south. As shown in the chart below, Match’s consolidated Q4 operating margins clocked in at 38%, flat sequentially but down 4 points y/y.

The drag here is Hinge, whose 30% segment operating margin declined 5 points sequentially and 4 points y/y. Again, this reflects the cannibalization nature of Match’s business. It’s investing remarkably into both marketing and product engineering for its most successful growth brand, which is a natural move. But driving growth benefits for Hinge also seems to be sapping growth from Tinder, which has long operated more profitably at a ~50% operating margin.

Valuation and key takeaways

The only saving grace that Match can boast as an investment is its valuation. At current share prices near $32, Match trades at a market cap of $8.23 billion. After we net off the $966.0 million of cash and sizable $3.85 billion debt pile on Match’s latest balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $11.15 billion.

For the current fiscal year, Match’s outlook calls for $3.375-$3.500 billion in revenue (-3% to flat y/y, which is below the company’s 3-year growth CAGR target of 4-6%, and yet another red flag) and $1.00-$1.03 billion in FCF, or 13-17% FCF growth (in line with the company’s target to deliver $3+ billion of FCF over three years).

Now, I’m skeptical of this FCF target as well - it’s unclear how the company expects to hit FCF expansion when revenue is not expected to exceed flat y/y, and when more of the company’s revenue mix is expected to shift out of Tinder and into Hinge, where the latter has a 20-point operating margin disadvantage.

Still, if we take the company’s estimates at face value, Match trades at:

  • 3.2x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 11.2x EV/FY25 FCF

When the S&P 500 continues to sit at a >20x P/E multiple, paying an ~11x multiple for Match’s FCF is enticing. At the same time, I find many risks to Match’s ability to hit its targets, especially as total payers decline and both growth and margins stall in Match’s best performing app, Hinge.

To me, this is a simple value trap: steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: GLIN: Getting Ahead Of The Indian Cycle
data.description:
Pressure on Indian equities has only worsened since the back half of last year and as a result, the growth-focused, ‘higher beta’ VanEck’s India Growth Leaders ETF (NYSEARCA:GLIN), has given up most of its 2024 gains since I last covered the fund (see GLIN: A Dip-Buying Opportunity Emerges In India).

At the heart of market concerns is an economic trifecta of slower growth, ‘sticky’ inflation, and below-par government outlays, all of which have spilled over into mass foreign outflows and poor quarterly earnings numbers as well. With earnings multiples also down a few turns (~21x forward for MSCI India), investors appear to be pricing in a slower ‘new normal’ and a sustained impact on corporate earnings power from here.

The question then is whether the Indian growth story has structurally slowed down or is merely going through a cyclical downswing. Recent data points suggest the latter as the more likely scenario. For one, inflation has already decelerated sharply (down over 60bps to a five-month low in January), allowing the central bank space to kickstart its rate cut cycle.

Meanwhile, this year’s Budget, while not quite as big on capex, did see some timely consumption-boosting measures, which, combined with receding food inflation, should boost growth in the coming months.

Tactically, the post-Budget setup looks good for GLIN’s portfolio, which currently skews in the right direction sectorally (overweight financials and consumers over capex-dependent industrials). And if India's nominal GDP does regain its +10% trend, still the best of the major global economies, Indian large-cap corporates should have little issue regaining their mid to high-teens % earnings growth trajectory (higher for the more economically sensitive small/mid-caps).

Having led the way down in recent months, GLIN’s growth-oriented portfolio, currently priced at 25x forward P/E (vs +27.8% one-year EPS growth), stands out as a rebound candidate when the cycle turns.

GLIN Overview – Fundamentals Intact

Fundamentally, not a lot has changed since last quarter for GLIN - both with regard to its 'GARP' (short for Growth at a Reasonable Price) approach and to its access to India's 'all-cap' (i.e., large, mid, and small-cap) equity universe. GLIN also continues to track the free-float market cap weighted MarketGrader India All-Cap Growth Leaders Index, which comprises 80 of the “most fundamentally sound companies with the best prospects in India”, as determined by MarketGrader.

Unlike the other 'all-cap' India alternative, WisdomTree’s India Earnings Fund ETF (EPI), GLIN’s stock selection is multi-pronged ('Growth' (revenue and earnings), 'Value' (forward multiples), and 'Quality' (profitability and cash flow)) and a lot more complex than your average profitability-focused filter.

But GLIN also comes with slightly higher fees (1.09% gross; 0.87% net expense ratio). And execution, as reflected in its improved but still relatively wide bid/ask spread, is an issue. Thus, investors do need to be comfortable underwriting some outperformance, over and above these added costs, from the fund's ‘GARP’ strategy.

Sources: WisdomTree, VanEck

GLIN Portfolio – Puts and Takes Ahead of the Next Rebalance

GLIN tracks an index with a lot of churn, so the fund's semi-annual rebalancing events tend to be quite significant. The last one, in September, for instance, saw twenty-seven out of the ~80-stock portfolio reshuffled. Given how much Indian valuations and growth expectations have shifted over the last six months, the next one, due in March, could be a particularly significant event.

Source: MarketGrader

For now, though, GLIN's sector breakdown leans toward beneficiaries of ongoing regime shifts. Financials, the current largest allocation at 26.4%, stands to benefit from monetary and regulatory easing by the central bank (see HDFC Bank: Asia's Compounding Machine Is Back On Track). Meanwhile, third-largest Consumer Discretionary (14.7%) stands out as a beneficiary of the government’s growing shift toward boosting consumption. Last but not least is GLIN’s second-largest exposure, Information Technology (19.4%), and its leverage to a pickup in IT budgets globally (see Infosys: Quality Large-Cap Levered To An IT Spending Recovery).

As for the single-stock split, there have been a few changes since last quarter. At the top, ICICI Bank (IBN) is now the largest holding at 6.0%, displacing IT services company HCL Technologies (second-largest holding at 5.7%). The rest of the top holdings list is similarly dominated by banks (State Bank of India at 5.6%) and IT services (Infosys (INFY) at 5.1% and Tata Consultancy Services at 5.0%).

Other notable shifts include GLIN’s increased small-cap exposure (up over two percentage points to 5.2%) at the expense of mid (13.9%) and large-caps (80.5%).

GLIN Performance – Down but Not Out

Deploying a growth-oriented strategy tends to work well in bull markets but, as the last quarter has shown, not so much in bear markets. Per GLIN’s latest disclosures, the fund is down -8.6% in NAV terms year-to-date and up only +2.0% over the last year – slightly behind key peer EPI’s +3.7% one-year return. The more defensive of the Indian 'factor' ETFs, Invesco’s large-cap-focused India ETF (see PIN: Riding Out The Indian Storm), has been the key one-year outperformer at +4.1%.

Zooming out, though, GLIN has still compounded at a respectable +5.9% and +6.6% pace over the last three and five years, respectively. GLIN has, however, lagged behind both EPI and PIN’s more straightforward stock selection strategies since tracking the MarketGrader India All Cap Growth Leaders Index in 2020.

Given its underwhelming track record, why then invest in GLIN over EPI or PIN? Ultimately, it comes down to the process; specifically, buying Indian growth without overpaying for it (i.e., 'GARP').

GLIN's headline portfolio stats, compiled by MarketGrader, make this clear – investors pay a lower P/E (~25x trailing) for more growth (~35% three-year average; ~28% one-year) and returns on equity (~18%). The fund’s growth orientation also allows it to outperform in upcycles (like in 2023/H1 2024), so those looking to express a more optimistic view on the Indian cycle will find more to like here.
